comments.title: Missing pants
comments.content:
Okay, input from anyone appreciated. This is probably way below JMG's pay grade. My mental health is still fine, but ...

Sorting and organising clothes is an endless huge job in my house, which nobody but me does. A few months ago I put aside four pairs of pants in a particular location knowing that they would be needed by a different member of the household in the near future. I know exactly where I put them, what they were, who they were for, and why. So last night the time came to tell that person that there were four suitable pairs of pants available. I told him, went to the third drawer down on the right side in that bedroom and - the drawer was empty. And believe me, we do NOT have empty drawers or cupboards in our house. And it's not as if the pants are anywhere else pants might go. Or as if anyone but me would bother to take responsibility for clothes. And the thought of a human intruder walking all the way through the house to that bedroom and then just taking four pairs of pants is preposterous. SO ... I have to sort of conclude that this may have been done by non-humans. I know how crazy this sounds. But any advice, guidance, courses of action, motives etc. would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content: That's really quite odd...but such things do happen, from time to time.

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content:
This might not help you much, but quite some years back, an older relative of mine packed some leftover green bean salad into one of these plastic food storage containers and then into the fridge. When she wanted to take it out the next day, container and bean salad were gone.

There wasn‘t anyone else in the household to have taken it (her husband wasn‘t prone to doing any work in the kitchen, or to preparing his own meals, for that matter), the fridge was a very clean and sorted fridge, there were no other people in the flat at that time, and it didn’t show up anywhere else, not in the garbage or any other places - and she and several other people took the kitchen and the whole flat inside out in the next few days. (And it‘s not like two mentally sane people would both hallucinate something like leftover green bean salad if there hadn‘t been any.)

This was a big thing for her back then, because for a while, she was very worried that her mind would start to fail and she‘d get dementia or whatnot. We‘ve never been able to clear this up, and they‘ve both been dead now for quite a few years.

So this has become something like the proverbial bean salad for me, whenever something has „unexplainedly“ gone missing - but so far, everything else has just turned out to have been misplaced in some way.

Unfortunately, I’ve still got no clue who or what took that bean salad away back then, so make of that what you will…

(Foodstuffs and pants… Hm. what is coming to mind is somebody who is on the road and will only take what they need at that time - but then why would somebody who operates on an unseen level need pants or food? And why four trousers at once?? :D )

As an aside, has that drawer been empty when you put in the pants, or has there been other stuff in there? If the latter, I‘m wondering what has happened to the other stuff?

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content: I believe the Catholic solution to problems like this is a prayer to St. Anthony, followed by a suitable thanks offering if/when the items turn up.

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content: ...the Orthodox version of this is to pray to St. Phanourios (who may or may not be a real saint, but who is extremely helpful for finding lost things!), and when the item turns up, there is a cake or sweet bread to bake and bring to church to share, called a Phanouropita.

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content: If I may interject with something frivolous, this does rather remind me of a particularly silly episode of South Park entitled "Underpants Gnomes"... maybe the writers were speaking from personal experience?

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content:
What a great thread, and I am so glad someone mentioned the Underpants Gnomes because that is EXACTLY what I thought about when I read it.

It sounds like in both cases of the Mystery of the Missing Pants and the Vanishing Green Bean Salad that the house spirits wanted offerings, did not get them, and simply took what they wanted. For revert2mean, since this form of etheric labor so often falls to you, you can start by thanking the items as you care for them and fold them and also thank the washer and dryer for their hard work. This alone will go a long way in making the spirits of the house happier. It also sounds like you have too many clothes and that the spirits are trying to tell you to reduce and minimize the amount of clothing you have. You might consider making a big donation of unused clothing with the resolution not to amass clothing that is undervalued, taken for granted, or rarely worn.

As far as the bean salad, it sounds as if the brownies coveted it because it was delicious and spirited it away to their realm. Hopefully they enjoyed it lol

replies.title: Re: Missing pants
replies.content:
I’d ask them to please return the Tupperware. Especially if they’re the ones who take all those containers and lids that mysteriously vanish, they can certainly stand to return one now and again.

Adding to the stories, let’s not forget Girl’s Gone Glasses 👓 , in which my glasses disappeared for 4 months. Even Saint Anthony couldn’t find them, or so I gathered from repeated appeals doing no good. Then one day I was kneeling on my bed, replacing a fitted sheet corner that had popped off, and my glasses hit the back of my heel in such a way that they could only have dropped from the ceiling. (If you’re wondering how I knew, no, I’m not psychic, it’s just that nothing else feels like a pair of glasses dropped from the ceiling onto your heel.). It would be marked understatement to say this was startling.

—Princess Cutekitten

- Cicada Grove

comments.title: Death
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I have stopped feeling sad that people are dying before their time as I have learned that life goes on in the afterlife and people get as many chances as they need to learn what they need.

Most of this I have learned from you, directly and indirectly, yet I notice that people dying still makes you sad. Are you sad for their families who are left behind to grieve and make do without valued members of the family and similar? Or, are you sad for the people who are dying? If you are sad for the dying, why is that? Is there something fearsome about death that I do not yet understand?
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content: It's partly a matter of the people who are left, who so often end up lonely and grieving, but death can be a wrench -- especially if you're young and had hopes for the rest of the life that's suddenly torn away from you. It's not that it's fearsome, it's as though you suddenly got dragged onto an airplane and flown to the other side of the world, knowing that you probably won't be able to contact the people you leave behind and may have to wait many years before you see them again. That's not the end of the world, but it can be a harsh thing to go through.

replies.title: Re: Death
replies.content:
Thank you for explaining that. I expect our prayers can comfort the departed and those left alone.

I have a weekly phone date with an older, blind friend and she says that is a big help to her. She likes it so much that she has a weekly phone date with another older lady from her macular degeneration group. Once, she was a couple of minutes late in calling her friend and her friend said in a panic, "I thought you were not going to call."

So, I urge everyone who reads this to make a weekly-phone date with a friend or relative who could use the moral support. A lot of the time, people just want to tell someone how their week went.
Maxine

To everyone who participates in these weekly prayer lists-- both those who pray, and those who request and accept prayer-- my heartfelt thanks for joining in. 

There's now a new optional feature to the weekly prayer list.

After some discussion on the matter, a sizable majority of the prayer list participants who made their voices heard agreed: we are of the opinion that when neither privacy nor modesty is a concern, it is generally more efficacious for the requesters of prayer to provide their real names, location, and even pictures of themselves. This both makes it easier for those praying to focus their intentions, and it requires the prayer requester to put some "skin in the game", opening the gates to more easily let in the powers who answer prayer.

Please continue to only share what information you are comfortable sharing of your personal information with the main prayer list, just as you have already been doing, and rest assured that your prayers will be heard.

comments.title: What is love?
comments.content:
Hi!

For a naive question: what is love, (from an occult perspective that is)?

I can have empathy for people and nature but love! That I find difficult to comprehend. It seems such a loaded word much abused and misunderstood.
Any insights welcomed.
Many thanks for your patience and erudition.

replies.title: Re: What is love?
replies.content:
The thing that makes this question difficult to answer is that the word "love" is a dumpster in which a vast number of very different emotional states and relationships have been shoved. In ancient Greek, for example, what we call "love" was divided up into a series of different feelings. Eros is sexual love; storge is love between family members, as parents for children; philia is the love that exists in friendship; agape is unselfish and unconditional love.

From an occult perspective no two of these have the same nature or mean the same thing. It's a complex subject, too -- entire books have been written about the occult dimensions of love.

replies.title: Re: What is love?
replies.content:
As a start, I would define love as “outgoing unselfish concern for the person (pet, whatever) in question. And of course Saint Paul covered the subject pretty thoroughly.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: What is love?
replies.content: That's agape -- I'm pretty sure that's the word Paul used, in fact. Eros is something rather different!

replies.title: Re: What is love?
replies.content: One definition I liked was "Love is when the happiness of the other person is as important to you as your own."

a) Systema is a Russian martial art; though I'm not sure if that makes it more accessible or less appealing in Poland. The Ryabko line is deeply concerned with the life force (they insist on calling it breath). The Kadochnikov line is clearly aware of it, but they talk about it in terms of concentration and imagination. What I've seen of Systema Spetsnaz (Wadim Starov's work), gives me no reason to think they are competent and much reason to think otherwise.

b) Historical European Martial Arts (HEMA) is in an incredibly awkward position in regards to this issue. The old books that they train out of explicitly discuss the spirit and the humors, but the modern practitioners I've encountered are very casual to dismiss such things as pious nonsense and superstition. If the situation is better in Poland, that could provide an excellent opportunity.

c) The early stages of the modern arts that fed into MMA regularly discussed the life force indirectly in older books on the subject. Boxing manuals once talked about breathing into ones strikes. Wrestling books talked about feeling the other person's weight and shifting your own around in ways that make it very clear they aren't talking about simple mass. Physical culture texts include discussions of circulation that wouldn't be out of place in a work on traditional chinese medicine.

An interesting example, the most magically capable martial artist I've known was a wrestler who ended up getting into Rosicrucianism because he felt like that was the best way to learn about the weirder side of wrestling that he enjoyed so much. He's made comments that implied there was some overlap between Rosicrucianism and dueling societies in times now past.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings everyone including JMG,

Had a couple of questions:
1) How do i dispose a couple of renaissance talismans (sun and venus)? I bought them from C Warnock; despite many consecrations, they don't seem to do anything as far as I can tell but I want to be respectful anyway.

2) What does the occult and spiritual traditions have to say about heaven? Is it merely a metaphor for an immanent reality or a real place (probably on another plane)?

Given that buddhists have reincarnation present even in some heavens (perhaps lower heavens), that probably is an astral idea? Not sure a non-reincarnating heaven beyond time fits.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) I'd advise that you give them to someone who wants them. Do it without asking any payment in return, because that cuts the connection to you.

2) The concept of "heaven" as it occurs in Western religious traditions is a jumbling together of two things. The first is that when you die, where you spend the time between lives depends on what we might as well call your moral state. You go to a part of the astral plane that resonates with the condition of your astral body, because like seeks like, and because you need to process the experiences of your life in an astral state that approximates your own astral body's condition. So if you spent your life pursuing selfish ends, abusing other people, and wallowing in crass desires, you spend much of the time between lives on the lower astral, which is pretty miserable -- it's the basis of beliefs about hell. (It doesn't help that you get to review your life as it was, not as you tried to convince yourself that it was) On the other hand, if you spent your life trying your best to follow the teachings of your faith, treating other people kindly, and developing a rich spiritual life, you spend your time instead on the upper astral, which is as pleasant as the lower astral is unpleasant. Again, that's where you need to be in order to process your memories of the life just ended -- and you have the benefit of experiencing just how much you made other people's lives better, by the way.

That's the first thing that gives rise to the idea of heaven. Then there's what happens when you have finally outgrown the need for a material body, and have established a body on the mental plane. At the end of the life where you do that, you don't go into the standard cycle of reincarnation again, you ascend; your material body dies but your other bodies remain intact, along with your personality, and you proceed to a different mode of existence in which many of the hard necessities of material existence no longer affect you. (For example, you no longer need to die and lose all your memories.)

The first of these is temporary, the second is permanent. Neither one is beyond time -- you have to travel a lot further up the path of spiritual evolution before you outgrow time! But conventional beliefs about heaven are based on muddled understandings of these two things.

replies.title: Re: Rising Call?
replies.content:
No, I haven't done a followup post -- I've been up to my eyeballs in other projects. (Notably getting the Essene work ready to release in PDF format.)

The Rising Call exercise, for those readers who missed it, is given here:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/222956.html

What it does is quite simple. Your brain basically floats in a pool of cerebrospinal fluid, which is held in by a set of membranes on the inside of your skull. A lot of brain chemicals flow through that fluid as it circulates. As we get old, however, the membranes get less resilient and the movement of the fluid basically settles into a rut; that's problematic because it keeps some brain chemicals from getting where they're supposed to go -- and that, in turn, makes it more difficult to practice certain esoteric disciplines that work with the pineal and pituitary glands. The Rising Call basically gives an internal massage to the membranes, by gently increasing the pressure on the fluid and then decreasing it again. Do it daily and your brain gets properly bathed in the fluid, the brain chemicals get where they need to go, and the practices that focus on those two glands can function unimpaired.

comments.title: Franz Bardon; Witch of Criswell (SPOILER ALERT!)

Good morning JMG and everybody else,

I hope you all had a pleasant weekend and a good start into the week! :-) JMG, thanks again for hosting this valuable space and answering questions.

I‘ve got two questions today, if I may, both stemming from me reading Franz Bardon‘s Initiation into Hermetics:

1. In the book, Bardon teaches a technique to „create“ elementals (as helpers of the mage).

(I‘ve read the German version which distinguishes between „Elemental“ and „Elementar“. The latter are the more advanced and more powerful, and I‘m mainly asking about them - not sure how they translate into English, though.)

There are different ways for a mage to create such an elemental: In the simplest way by „condensing“ an elemental energy, in more complex forms by doing the same, but also creating an astral and mental body etc. (The whole process description is actually quite creepy.)

Now, if I understood Bardon correctly, he seems to suggest that the mage actually „creates“ these elementals, i.e. not only that they are living entities with their own will (e.g. he warns the mage that his elementals might try to threaten or wheedle him into not destroying them, which assumes they have a will and desires of their own), but his choice of words also suggests that these are not „existing“ entities which are called by the mage, or something along those lines, but that the mage actually creates them (somewhat literally in some cases) out of thin air.

So… I didn‘t think (and still don‘t think, somehow), that human beings can actually „create“ the „soul“ of other beings. Making a baby, physically, is one thing - creating the soul which will „inhabite“ it is something else entirely.

I.e. did I get Bardon wrong here? Or am I mistaken? Or was he? Or what else is going on here??

Does occultism show or assume that humans can literally create other beings, including their soul, desires, …?

2. SPOILER ALERT Witch of Criswell

First, I‘d like to state that the next question is purely my curious mind trying to sort out different levels of being, and of entities…

In the Witch of Criswell, the villain had that nasty entity at her service for many years, at least that is what the story suggests.

However, Bardon advises the mage against keeping elementals for years, because he thinks that the longer one keeps them, the harder it will be to destroy them at the end, and also the more powerful they might become and thus take control over the mage.

If the witch of Criswell has kept hers for years, then is that because it‘s some other form of entity (and if so, in which way is it different from Bardon‘s elementals?)? Or because she has used other means to control it (again, different in which way?)? Or would she have switched out her „servant“ from time to time to a new one?

….

Frankly, re-reading that passage in Bardon today in order to phrase the question left me with even more of a creepy feeling than the first time.

If I got him right, and if what he writes is true… who would create a „living“, conscious being, just as a commodity to be used, and then kill it once it has done its job? That‘s truly baffling - and part of me hopes I misread him…

I originally picked up the Initiation into Hermetics as an evening read just to broaden my occult horizon a bit - not sure I wanted it to be that broad, tbh…

Milkyway, still puzzled

comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

I have a question that touches on the gray area between magic and psychology. People often find that connecting things they want to do with their identity and sense of self can help them achieve more--for example, if you want to get better about exercising regularly, it can help to think of yourself as the kind of person who stays active and makes time to work out; you may be less likely to drink if you think of yourself as someone who doesn't drink, etc.

I've also seen the negative side of this more times than I can count, such as with people who identify so strongly with their traumas and mental health challenges that they cannot let them go ("that's just how I am, this is who I am, etc ad nauseam). Similarly I think many people would be better off avoiding natal astrology simply because they can get over invested in their sign to the detriment of deeper self-knowledge.

With all that being said, what kind of role do you think identity plays or should play in the study of magic? Since the various western mystic traditions seem more interested in balancing the ego rather than dissolving it, I would assume there's a useful place for it to some degree or another. Should positive affirmations, for example, be part of early magical practice?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Identity is a crutch. A crutch can be useful, especially when you're just learning how to walk with a damaged leg, but the goal should be to do without it as soon as you reasonably can. Thus I teach the use of affirmations, but I also encourage people to practice journaling and the practices of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which are intended to replace identity with self-knowledge.

replies.title: Re: Question about Spirits of the Elements
replies.content:
Sure. The elemental spirits are "creations of the created" -- they didn't come into existence the way we did, as souls descending from the higher planes to gain experience in the world of matter. comments.title: Euthanasia
comments.content:
Hello JMG, thank you for providing us another opportunity to ask you a question.

I wonder about euthanasia. If someone is severely suffering without any chance of healing (like someone suffering from ALS), what would be the consequences if they opt for euthanasia (provided it is legal in their country)?

replies.title: Re: Euthanasia
replies.content:
Suicide is suicide; it means that you're going to be killing time until your body would have died in the ordinary course of things. If that's a better prospect than severe suffering, as of course it can be, that's a choice you can make.

On the other hand, these days -- especially but not only in Canada -- "euthanasia" is turning into a code word for state-sanctioned medical murder of the unhealthy and undesirable. That's not suicide -- the fact that you signed a paper doesn't change the fact that a bureaucracy bullied you into it by threatening to deprive you of all welfare benefits when you're too sick to work. That's murder, and the people who commit it will have some very unwelcome karma waiting for them.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You'll have to ask someone better versed in Indian tradition if that's the way they understand things. That's not at all the way Western occultism sees it -- and it also doesn't fit my past life memories or those of anyone else I know of with whom I've discussed such things. Social class is just one of many details of the life into which you are born, and those are whatever they need to be in order to give you the best opportunity to deal with your karma. In my case, my most recent three lives (including this one) have been relentlessly middle-class; the one before that, I was born into extreme poverty in a slum in 19th-century Dublin, and died young; the one before that I was the son of a wealthy but not titled English landowner; the time before that, I was the daughter of a relatively poor Fen Country farmer, and lived and died a farmer's daughter, wife, and mother. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very piecemeal, a few glimpses at a time. It was, as I recall, about four years after I started daily meditation and other magical practices that I got my first past life memory, an odd little flash of sitting in an overstuffed chair and smoking a pipe while wearing a silk smoking jacket. I don't smoke, and loathe the smell of tobacco, but in the memory I liked the flavor of the pipe smoke very much, and remembered how to pull the smoke into my mouth but not breathe it into my lungs. I sat with that memory, and bit by bit other traces of that life followed. Others came later. I still get new memories tolerably often.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If by Indian tradition you mean Hinduism, then your understanding is correct and this is an extant teaching in some (but not all) schools and traditions. One of the quirks of the Indian tradition (and I'm including Sikhism and Buddhism too; though it seems to happen the most with Hindus) is a tendency to take the teachings of one particular school or sect and claim they are universal. Westerners who want to write about dharmic traditions seem to get fooled into this quite often, but they are also quite happy to do it to themselves.

A discrete but superficially similar teaching is that a virtuous life will lead to a more auspicious rebirth, which it looks like you also referenced. This doesn't mean that good karma necessarily leads to a higher caste / social class, just that wholesome activities will bring wholesome results, and one of those possible results is a more pleasant next life. This interacts with a separate teaching (common to the west) that patiently dealing with suffering is also virtuous.

Personally, I think we are dropped into whatever social class is most conducive to the kinds of lessons we need to learn. One of the reasons I hold the idea cultivated soul -> higher social class in such low regard is because my first human incarnation I was a member of the upper class.

replies.title: Re: What is the difference between spirituality and magic?
replies.content: It depends on how you define the words, of course. I tend to define "magic" as "the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will." replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a fine tangle, because people in different traditions have used the words "soul" and "spirit" in a gaggle of mutually incompatible ways!

Yes, I'm familiar with the Greek usage, but the traditions I work with don't follow that. In those traditions, the spirit comprises all the aspects of the self that have not been developed yet into bodies -- the seeds that will eventually become the spiritual, causal, and divine bodies of each of us in the far future. It represents the core of what passes from life to life. The soul, by contrast, is what we've gained by our previous incarnations -- the qualities of character, talent, conscience, and awareness generated by all those lives.

The occult traditions I've studied don't have a lot of discussion of specific kinds of imbalance, just a clear sense that we all have some such burden and have to get working on balancing things out.

2) Regular practice of meditation, divination, and journaling will develop those skills.

3) Yes, but balance it with workings invoking Saturn and the Moon so you don't become an egomaniac.

While I'm not claiming *competence* at any of this stuff, at least my foot and hand reflexology results are coherent (the only things I really feel are the base toe/finger joints, which - fits what the material says) - but in the ears, I'm not sure about what I should be doing that'd cause me to feel a damn thing (squeezing around hasn't, I think).

replies.content: The material in the Essene papers is very incomplete when it comes to ear reflexology. I find it works well to start by rubbing the ear generally, front and back, and then pinch and squeeze every part of the ear starting at the bottom of the lobe and working back, around, and up from there. But it may be helpful to see if some other sources can be found.
